audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a man who works at an employment agency, phoning a woman who is looking for a job.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, hello. Is that Julie Davison?
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: This is Greg Preston from the Employment Agency.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: We met last week when you came in to enquire about office work.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Oh, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Now we've just had some details come in of a job which might interest you.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK, so this is a position for a receptionist.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: I believe you've done that sort of work before.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yes, I have.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I worked in a sports center for a couple of years before I got married and had the children.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Well, this job's in Fordham, so not too far away for you, and it's at the medical center there.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK, so where exactly is that?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: It's quite near the station on Chastons Road.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Sorry?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Chastons Road.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That's CHASTONS.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: So what would the work involve?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Dealing with enquiries from patients?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And you'd also be involved in making appointments, whether face to face or on the phone, and rescheduling them if necessary.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: That shouldn't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: And another of your duties would be keeping the center's database up-to-date.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Then you might have other general administrative duties as well.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: But those would be the main ones.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, when the details came in, I immediately thought of you, because one thing they do require is someone with experience.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And you did mention your work at the sports center when you came in to see us.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yes, in fact, I enjoyed that job.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Is there anything else they're looking for?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, they say it's quite a high-pressure environment.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: They're always very busy, and patients are often under stress.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: So they want someone who can cope with that.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And stay calm, and at the same time, be confident when interacting with the public.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Well, after dealing with three children all under 5, I reckon I can cope with that.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I'm sure you can.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And then another thing they mention is that they're looking for someone with good IT skills...
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Not a problem.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: So you'd be interested in following this up?
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: When would it start?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, they're looking for someone from the beginning of next month.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: But I should tell you that this isn't a permanent job.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: It's temporary.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: So the contract would be just to the end of September, but they do say that there could be further opportunities after that.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK, and what would the hours be?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, they want someone who can start at a quarter to eight in the morning.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Could you manage that?
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yes, my husband would have to get the kids up and off to my mother's.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: She's going to be looking after them while I'm at work.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: What time would I finish?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: One fifteen.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That should work out all right.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I can pick the kids up on my way home, and then I'll have the afternoon with them.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, one thing.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Is there parking available for staff at the center?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Yes, there is.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And it's also on a bus route.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, I expect I'll have the car, but it's good to know that.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: OK, so where do I go from here?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, if you're happy for me to do so, I'll forward your CV and references.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And then the best thing would probably be for you to phone them, so they can arrange for an interview.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Bye now.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Other information

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a guide at a farming museum talking to a group of visitors.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone, and welcome to the Museum of Farming Life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I understand it's your first visit here, so I'd like to give you some background information about the museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then explain a little about what you can see during your visit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, where we're standing at the moment is the entrance to a large building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That was constructed in 1880, as the home of a local businessman, Alfred Palmer, of the Palmer Biscuit Factory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was later sold, and became a hall of residence for students in 1911, and a museum in 1951.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 2005, a modern extension was built to accommodate the museum's collections.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The museum's owned by the university, and apart from two rooms that are our offices, the university uses the main part of the building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may see students going into the building for lessons, but it's not open to museum visitors, I'm afraid.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a shame, because the interior architectural features are outstanding.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Especially the room that used to be the library.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Luckily, we've managed to keep entry to the museum free.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This includes access to all the galleries, outdoor areas, and the rooms for special exhibitions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We run activities for children and students, such as the Museum Club, for which there's no charge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We do have a donation box just over there, so feel free to give whatever amount you consider appropriate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We do have a cloakroom.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you'd like to leave your coats and bags somewhere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike other museums, photography is allowed here, so you might like to keep your cameras with you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You might be more comfortable not carrying around heavy rucksacks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though keep your coats and jackets on, as it's quite cold in the museum garden today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I'd like to tell you about the different areas of the museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Just inside and outside the main gallery, we have an area called Four Seasons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here, you can watch a four-minute animation of a woodland scene.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was designed especially for the museum by a group of young people on a film studies course, and it's beautiful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Children absolutely love it, but then so do adults.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main gallery's called Town and Country. It includes a photographic collection of prize-winning sheep and shepherds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Leaving Town and Country, you enter Farmhouse Kitchen, which is... well, self-explanatory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here we have the oldest collection of equipment for making butter and cheese in the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this morning, a specialist cheesemaker will be giving demonstrations of how it's produced.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may even get to try some.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After that, you can go in two directions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To the right is a staircase that takes you up to a landing, from where you can look down on the galleries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To the left is a room called A Year on the Farm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There is lots of seating here, as sometimes we use the room for school visits, so it's a good place to stop for a rest.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you're feeling competitive, you can take our memory test, in which you answer questions about things you've seen in the museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The next area is called Wagon Walk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This contains farm carts from nearly every part of the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's surprising how much regional variation there was.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Beside the carts are display boards with information about each one.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The carts are old and fragile, so we ask you to keep your children close to you, and ensure they don't climb on the carts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From Wagon Walk, you can either make your way back to reception, or go out into the garden, or even go back to take another look in the galleries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the far corner of the garden is Bees are Magic, but we're redeveloping this area, so you can't visit that at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can still buy our honey in the shop though.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finally, there is the pond, which contains all kinds of interesting wildlife.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are baby ducks that are only a few days old, as well as tiny frogs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The pond isn't deep, and there is a fence around it, so it's perfectly safe for children.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The carts are old and fragile, so we ask you to keep your children close to you, and ensure they don't climb on the carts.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: From Wagon Walk, you can either make your way back to reception, or go out into the garden, or even go back to take another look in the galleries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the far corner of the garden is Bees are Magic, but we're redeveloping this area, so you can't visit that at the moment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can still buy our honey in the shop though.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Finally, there is the pond, which contains all kinds of interesting wildlife.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are baby ducks that are only a few days old, as well as tiny frogs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The pond isn't deep, and there is a fence around it, so it's perfectly safe for children.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The university uses part of the museum building as
What does the guide say about the entrance fee?
options: Visitors decide whether or not they wish to pay.
Only children and students receive a discount.
The museum charges extra for special exhibitions.

items.title: What are visitors advised to leave in the cloakroom?

text: What information does the speaker give about each of the following areas of the museum?
textReadable: What information does the speaker give about each of the following areas of the museum?

list: There are new things to see.
This is only for school groups.
There is a quiz for visitors.
It features something created by students.
body.title: Areas of museum

audioscript.text: Part 3, following instructions from their tutor, you will hear two trainee teachers discussing the use of origami, a paper folding activity in the classroom.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So now I want you to discuss the lesson we've just been watching on the video, and think about the ways in which origami can be a useful educational tool.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Can you all work with the person sitting next to you?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I had no idea that such a simple thing like folding squares of paper to make the shape of something like a bird could be such an amazing tool.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: It's made me see origami in a whole new light.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: It was interesting to see the educational skills the children were developing by doing origami.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: On the video you could see them really listening hard to make sure they did all the steps in the right order to make the bird.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: In this lesson, they were working individually, but it would also be interesting to see if the children could work out how to make something simple, uh, without being given any direction.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: That would help with building teamwork as well.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Yes, but much more of a challenge.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: One thing that really stood out for me was that the children were all having fun while being taught something new.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Which is a key aim of any lesson with this age group.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: And although these kids had no problems with folding the paper, with younger children you could do origami to help practice fine motor skills.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Shall we talk about the individual children we saw on the video?
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: I wrote all their names down and took some notes.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Yes, I did too.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: OK, good.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Let's start with Sid.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: He was interesting, because before they started doing the origami, he was being quite disruptive.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Yes, he really benefited from having to use his hands.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: It helped him to settle down and start concentrating.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Yes, I noticed that too.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: What about Jack?
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: I noticed he seemed to want to work things out for himself.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: You could see him trying out different things rather than asking the teacher for help.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: What did you make of Naomi?
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: She seemed to be losing interest at one point, but then she decided she wanted her mouse to be the best.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: And that motivated her to try harder.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: She didn't seem satisfied with hers in the end, though.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Anya was such a star.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: She listened so carefully, and then produced the perfect bird with very little effort.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Hmm, I think the teacher could have increased the level of difficulty for her.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Hmm, maybe.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: I think it was the first time Zara had come across origami.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: She looked as if she didn't really get what was going on.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: She seemed unsure about what she was supposed to do.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: But in the end, hers didn't turn out too badly.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'm sure it was a positive learning experience for her.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I think one reason why the origami activity worked so well in this class was that the teacher was well prepared.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: I think it would have taken me ages to prepare examples showing each of the steps involved in making the bird.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: But that was a really good idea.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: The children could see what they were aiming for, and much better for them to be able to hold something, rather than just looking at pictures.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Hmm, those physical examples supported her verbal explanations really well.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: It's strange that origami isn't used more widely.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Why do you think that is?
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Well, teachers may just feel it's not that appealing to children who are used to doing everything on computers, especially boys.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Even if they're aware of the benefits.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Oh, I don't know.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: It's no different to any other craft activity.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: I bet it's because so many teachers are clumsy like me.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: That's true.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Too much effort required if you're not good with your hands.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Well, anyway, I think we should try it out in our maths teaching practice with Year 3.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: I can see using origami is a really engaging way of reinforcing children's knowledge of geometric shapes, like they were doing in the video.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: But I think it would also work really well for presenting fractions, which is coming up soon.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: That's something most of the kids in that class might struggle with.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: Origami would also be good practice for using symmetry, but I think they did that last term.
audioscript.speaker: LIA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: Well, let's try and get some ideas together, and plan the lesson next week.
audioscript.speaker: SEB

audioscript.text: OK, if you could all stop...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO educational skills were shown in the video of children doing origami?

text: Which comment do the students make about each of the following children in the video?
textReadable: Which comment do the students make about each of the following children in the video?

list: seemed to find the activity calming
seemed to find the activity easy
body.title: Children

items.title: Before starting an origami activity in class, the students think it is important for the teacher to
options: make models that demonstrate the different stages.
check children understand the terminology involved.
tell children not to worry if they find the activity difficult.

items.title: The students agree that some teachers might be unwilling to use origami inclass because
options: they may not think that crafts are important.
they may not have the necessary skills.
they may worry that it will take up too much time.

items.title: Why do the students decide to use origami in their maths teaching practice?

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a literature student giving a talk about Victor Hugo, a famous 19th century French writer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The person I've chosen to talk about is the French writer Victor Hugo.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many people have heard of him because his novel, Les Miserables,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: which he wrote in 1862, is famous around the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It became a stage musical in the 1980s, and a film version was also released in 2012.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So some of us, I'm sure, have a pretty general idea of the plot, but we know much less about the author.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, I'm going to provide a little more insight into this talented man.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I'm going to talk particularly about the home he had on the island of Guernsey in the British Channel Islands, but first, his early career.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As I've said, he was a writer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He was at the height of his career in Paris, and he was very highly regarded by his colleagues.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far as literature was concerned, he was the leading figure of the Romantic movement.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, as well as being a literary genius, he also gave many speeches about issues like the level of poverty in his society.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He felt very strongly about this, and about other areas where change was needed, like education.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This kind of outspoken criticism was not well liked by the rulers of France.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And eventually the emperor - Napoleon III - told Victor Hugo to leave Paris and not return.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In other words, he sent him into exile.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So Victor Hugo was forced to reside in other parts of Europe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Guernsey was actually his third place of exile.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And he landed there in 1855.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He produced a lot while on Guernsey, including Les Miserables.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And to do this, he had to spend a great deal of time in the home that he had there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was a property that he bought using the money he'd made in France, from the publication of a collection of his poetry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was the only property he ever owned, and he was very proud of it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The property Victor Hugo bought on Guernsey was a large five-storey house in the capital town of St Peter Port, and he lived there for 15 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Returning to France in 1870 when Napoleon's empire collapsed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He decorated and furnished each level or floor of the house in unique and wonderful ways, and many people consider the inside of the house to be a work of art.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, it's a museum that attracts 200,000 visitors a year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He lived in the house with his family, and portraits of its members still hang in rooms on the ground floor, along with drawings that he did during his travels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That he felt were important to him.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In other ground floor rooms, there are huge tapestries that he would have designed and loved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The walls are covered in dark wood panelling that Victor Hugo created himself using wooden furniture that he bought in the market.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The items were relatively inexpensive, and he used them to create intricate carvings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They gave an atmosphere on the lower level that was shadowy,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and rather solemn.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On the next level of the house, there are two impressive lounges where he entertained his guests.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One lounge has entirely red furnishings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as sofas and wall coverings, and the other blue.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's a strong Chinese influence in these areas, in things like the wallpaper pattern.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the lamps, which he would have made himself by copying original versions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: His library, where he left many of his favorite books, forms the hallway to the third floor, and was a comfortable area where he could relax and enjoy his afternoons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then, at the very top of the house, there's a room called the Lookout,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: called that because it looks out over the harbor.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In contrast to the rather dark lower levels, it's full of light, and was like a glass office where he would write until lunchtime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Often at his desk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So Victor Hugo was a man of many talents, but he was also true to his values.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While living in his house on Guernsey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He entertained many other famous writers, but he also invited a large group of local children from the deprived areas of the island to dinner once a week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, he served them their food, which was an extraordinary gesture for the time period.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1927, the house was owned by his relatives, and they decided to donate it to the city of Paris.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has since been restored using photographs from the period, and as I mentioned earlier, is now a museum that is open to the public.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Guernsey was actually his third place of exile.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And he landed there in 1855.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He produced a lot while on Guernsey, including Les Miserables.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And to do this, he had to spend a great deal of time in the home that he had there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was a property that he bought using the money he'd made in France, from the publication of a collection of his poetry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was the only property he ever owned, and he was very proud of it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The property Victor Hugo bought on Guernsey was a large five-storey house in the capital town of St Peter Port, and he lived there for 15 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Returning to France in 1870 when Napoleon's empire collapsed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He decorated and furnished each level or floor of the house in unique and wonderful ways, and many people consider the inside of the house to be a work of art.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today, it's a museum that attracts 200,000 visitors a year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He lived in the house with his family, and portraits of its members still hang in rooms on the ground floor, along with drawings that he did during his travels.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That he felt were important to him.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In other ground floor rooms, there are huge tapestries that he would have designed and loved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The walls are covered in dark wood panelling that Victor Hugo created himself using wooden furniture that he bought in the market.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The items were relatively inexpensive, and he used them to create intricate carvings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They gave an atmosphere on the lower level that was shadowy,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: and rather solemn.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On the next level of the house, there are two impressive lounges where he entertained his guests.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One lounge has entirely red furnishings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Such as sofas and wall coverings, and the other blue.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's a strong Chinese influence in these areas, in things like the wallpaper pattern.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the lamps, which he would have made himself by copying original versions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: His library, where he left many of his favorite books, forms the hallway to the third floor, and was a comfortable area where he could relax and enjoy his afternoons.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And then, at the very top of the house, there's a room called the Lookout,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: called that because it looks out over the harbor.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In contrast to the rather dark lower levels, it's full of light, and was like a glass office where he would write until lunchtime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Often at his desk.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So Victor Hugo was a man of many talents, but he was also true to his values.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: While living in his house on Guernsey.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He entertained many other famous writers, but he also invited a large group of local children from the deprived areas of the island to dinner once a week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's more, he served them their food, which was an extraordinary gesture for the time period.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1927, the house was owned by his relatives, and they decided to donate it to the city of Paris.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It has since been restored using photographs from the period, and as I mentioned earlier, is now a museum that is open to the public.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: It has been adapted for theatre and cinema.
We know more about its overall <input> than about its author.
items.title: His novel, Les Miserables

items: In Paris, his career was successful and he led the Romantic movement.
He spoke publicly about social issues, such as <input> and education.
Napoleon III disliked his views and exiled him.
items.title: His early career

items: Victor Hugo had to live elsewhere in <input>.
He used his income from the sale of some <input> he had written to buy a house on Guernsey.
items.title: His exile from France

items: Victor Hugo lived in this house until the end of the Empire in France.
The ground floor contains portraits, <input> and tapestries that he valued.
He bought cheap <input> made of wood and turned this into beautiful wall carvings.
The first floor consists of furnished areas with wallpaper and <input> that have a Chinese design.
The library still contains many of his favourite books.
He wrote in a room at the top of the house that had a view of the <input>.
He entertained other writers as well as poor <input> in his house.
Victor Hugo's <input> gave ownership of the house to the city of Parisin 1927.
items.title: His house on Guernsey
